content:
The idea of treating geometric quantities as objects you can add and scale dates back to work on forces and velocities in physics, but the modern **vector space** abstraction emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries when mathematicians unified geometry, algebra and analysis.

Grassmann and later Hilbert, Banach and others formalized vector spaces and infinite-dimensional extensions, making it possible to study functions, sequences and eventually high-dimensional data with the same tools used for arrows in the plane.

In the computer age, finite-dimensional vector spaces became the natural home for tabular data, images flattened into vectors, and later word embeddings and neural activations—so the abstract axioms now underpin virtually every numerical ML pipeline.

timeline.label: Geometric vectors
timeline.description: Forces and velocities in physics are combined by parallelogram addition, motivating vectors as more than isolated numbers.

timeline.label: Abstract vector spaces
timeline.description: Mathematicians define vector spaces axiomatically, separating the algebraic structure from any particular coordinate representation.

timeline.label: ML and embeddings
timeline.description: High-dimensional feature vectors and learned embeddings make vector-space reasoning central to modern machine learning and generative AI.
